points.text: Enable detailed request tracing (timestamp start/end) for checkout API to measure where latency spikes.
Log SQL query execution time and row count for the new join; compare before/after performance.
Add idempotency keys to checkout requests so retries don’t create duplicate charges.
Temporarily revert or disable the new join in a staging environment to isolate its impact on latency.
Monitor database lock/wait stats; look for deadlocks or table scans caused by the join.
Implement exponential backoff on client retries and cap max attempts to reduce duplicate submissions.
Run load tests simulating concurrent checkouts to reproduce the 5–10 s delay and double‑charge scenario.
Run EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) on the new join query to see actual row counts, index usage, and I/O cost.
Check that all join columns have matching indexes (e.g., foreign key columns) to avoid full table scans.
Add covering indexes on columns used in SELECT, WHERE, and ORDER BY of the checkout query to reduce lookups.
Verify the transaction isolation level; use REPEATABLE READ or SERIALIZABLE to prevent lost updates during retries.
Implement a unique constraint or idempotency key on the order/payment table to block duplicate charges on retry.
Wrap the entire checkout logic in a single atomic transaction; commit only after all steps succeed.
Monitor the slow‑query log and DB metrics (locks, deadlocks, CPU) during peak checkout to spot bottlenecks.
If the join pulls large tables, consider materializing a summary view or denormalizing frequently accessed fields.
Enable DB query logging and capture execution time for the new join to identify latency spikes.
Wrap the checkout operation in a single DB transaction with appropriate isolation (e.g., SERIALIZABLE).
Add a unique constraint or idempotency key on orders/payments to reject duplicate submissions.
Replace client‑side retries with server‑side retry logic using exponential backoff after confirming no pending transaction.
Run EXPLAIN on the new join query; add missing indexes to ensure the join is efficient.
Set up monitoring/alerts for duplicate order amounts or timestamps to detect double‑charge events promptly.
